SPE-220950-MS
Reference Synthetic Dataset for Drilling Inventory Optimization
O. E. Abdelaziem, Amal Petroleum Company, Egypt; R. M. Khafagy, BP, Egypt; H. M. Darwish, SLB, Saudi Arabia;
M. M. Abdelrahman, ADNOC Onshore, United Arab Emirates
Copyright 2024, Society of Petroleum Engineers DOI 10.2118/220950-MS
This paper was prepared for presentation at the SPE Annual Technical Conference and Exhibition held in New Orleans, Louisiana, USA, 23 - 25 September 2024.
This paper was selected for presentation by an SPE program committee following review of information contained in an abstract submitted by the author(s). Contents
of the paper have not been reviewed by the Society of Petroleum Engineers and are subject to correction by the author(s). The material does not necessarily reflect
any position of the Society of Petroleum Engineers, its officers, or members. Electronic reproduction, distribution, or storage of any part of this paper without the written
consent of the Society of Petroleum Engineers is prohibited. Permission to reproduce in print is restricted to an abstract of not more than 300 words; illustrations may
not be copied. The abstract must contain conspicuous acknowledgment of SPE copyright.
Summary
Data analytics holds immense importance in optimizing inventory management in the oilfield due to its
significant business impact. However, the progress of research is limited by restrictions of sharing inventory
data publicly, owing to privacy concerns. This paper introduces a novel synthetic dataset for drilling
inventory systems in oil-integrated companies, which shall offer a valuable open-source reference for
future studies. Additionally, diverse analytical approaches were employed to showcase their effectiveness
in leveraging cost reductions.
Generating synthetic datasets involves the implementation of data-driven and process-driven
methodologies. Given the inherent risk of endogeneity bias, a process-driven approach, combined with
domain knowledge, was followed. Primarily, pre-defined criteria were established to govern dataset
generation while accounting for various possible well templates. In essence, multiple constraints outlined
the relationship among oil prices, expected annual drilling activities, timing and cost of item receipts, and
the interdependence of item quantities and issuances. Next, controlled noise was introduced to account for
irregularities and periodic inclusion of cutting-edge items. Ultimately, diverse inventory scenarios were
subjected to analytics and optimization.
The introduced inventory dataset is characterized by randomness, rule-based procedure, and iterative
process. It allows replicability and adaptability to divergent layouts by virtue of user-defined parameters.
Overall, it possesses a history of transactions that extends over 20 years, exhibits an average asset value of 5
million USD, features around 500 unique stock keeping units (SKUs), and accommodates more than 15,000
transactions, such as receipts, issues, returns, sales, loans, inspections, and junks. Initial inventory volume
was estimated based on the drilling activities of a hypothetical medium-sized oil company that operates an
average of 10 drilling and workover wells per year. The generated dataset covers abundant specifications of
multidisciplinary SKUs, covering tubular goods, drilling accessories, completion equipment, and wellhead
materials. Items were selected delicately to represent diverse well configurations, encompassing sidetracks,
slim holes, gravel-packed wells, artificially lifted wells, and dual producers. Furthermore, probable mistakes
and human errors were taken into account, to improve the system’s reliability to closely mimic real-
world conditions. Subsequently, and upon investigating various Key Performance Indicators (KPIs), it was

observed that analytics and clustering techniques are promising in elucidating latent challenges, maximizing
profits, and achieving cost savings up to 10% of the inventory’s total value.
This paper presents a novel, openly accessible synthetic dataset for drilling inventory systems in oil-
integrated companies for the first time in literature, to the best of authors’ knowledge. The introduced dataset
was meticulously designed to ensure scalability and minimize drift. Positioned as a reference for future
research, this dataset aims to drive forward the advancement of research in analytics and machine learning,
to deliver ingenious solutions for inventory management optimization in digital oilfields.
Introduction
Inventory data is often restricted and unpublishable due to concerns about data privacy, competitive
advantage, intellectual property, regulatory compliance, and data misinterpretation, negatively impacting
research initiatives. Bellovin et al. (2019) demonstrated that synthetic data sets balance the tradeoff between
utility and privacy. El Emam et al. (2020) elaborated three types of synthetic data, which could be generated
from actual datasets, without using real data, or using a hybrid technique. Alnumay et al. (2022) revealed
the importance of generating synthetic data sets in oil industry to develop AI solutions as an alternative to
actual field data, especially when real data is restricted. Eldred et al. (2023) highlighted the significant, yet
under-researched potential of artificial intelligence (AI) in inventory management in oilfields and discerned
that data analytics and machine learning would be feasible only with appropriate dataset, which is defined as
the one containing representative information on drilling sequence data, stock data, and consumption data.
This research has two objectives: 1) majorly, to create a synthetic reference dataset for drilling inventory
without using real data, and 2) minorly, to show the role of analytics in inventory management and
optimization. The data set was generated based on simulation runs, and multidisciplinary domain knowledge
of supply chain in the oil industry along with drilling and completion engineering expertise, which
ascertained the development of resilient inventory system aligned with real-data and matches actual drilling
operations. This work shall serve as a benchmark for researchers to innovate and apply novel supply
chain mathematical models to optimize the holding inventory value in upstream operating companies.
The generated dataset is openly accessible, replicable, and available on https://github.com/OsamaEA/
SPE_220950_MS.
This paper is organized as follows. In the Introduction section, earlier work on the dual objectives
is demonstrated under preceding studies, followed by declaring the problem statement. The Inventory
Management Review section summarizes supply chain and inventory control problems. The Methodology
section details the workflow used to generate the synthetic dataset. In the Results section, a step-by-step
approach is followed to explore how the generated dataset mimics real inventory data. Under the Discussion
section, statistical insights and the role of analytics have been investigated on the introduced data set. In
the section of Limitations and Future work, possibilities for ongoing research are outlined. Finally, the
Conclusion section summarizes the key findings.
Preceding studies
It was reported that more than 89 articles in research have addressed SCM in oil and gas companies with
over 52 distinct SCM models being investigated (Alhosani et al. 2019). Inventory management in the oil
upstream industry faces numerous challenges, including unpredictable lead times, demand fluctuations,
biased forecasting, pipeline stock, SKU proliferation, and SKU distribution mix error. The ultimate objective
of research is to optimize the ratio of profitability.
Synthetic-Dataset-Based Inventory Analytics in Oil Upstream Industry.   Based on a synthetic data set,
Marcellino and Sichman (2010) proposed local optimization and multi-agent approaches for a supply chain,
which integrates three terminals of two refineries and a drilling location. Montagna and Cafaro (2019)
developed a framework to optimize SCM, using both a hypothetical illustrative scenario and a real case
Downloaded from http://onepetro.org/SPEATCE/proceedings-pdf/24ATCE/24ATCE/D031S042R003/3603481/spe-220950-ms.pdf/1 by Schlumberger Oilfield UK Plc user on 27 September 2025

from an oil and gas company in South America, with the objective of minimizing costs of inventory holding,
investments, and operations.
Inventory Analytics.   Ali et al. (2020) examined inventory controlling efficiency of an oil producing
company in terms of backorder and service levels. Their study, which included attributes of annual demand,
per unit cost, and lead time in days, was limited to a data set of the top 10 class A items. Laskar (2022)
showed that implementing analytics in smart warehouse management systems for onshore and offshore oil
company locations enhanced the overall supply chain performance with seamless integration with an ERP
system. Ahunanya et al. (2022) addressed inventory management systems in drilling fluid firms through a
questionnaire and statistical assessments to evaluate the relationship between various business metrics and
financial inefficiency in stock systems.
Synthetic Datasets Generation.   Bellovin et al. (2019) defined synthetic data as a sort of fake data derived
from real data, highlighting its value in mirroring real data distributions and being indistinguishable from it.
Jordon et al. (2022) defined synthetic data sets as those created using mathematical models or algorithms,
aiming at addressing data science problems. El Emam et al. (2020) introduced the concept of utility to
measure how well a synthetic dataset serves as a proxy for real data, stating that analysis results from
synthetic data should resemble outcomes from real data. They demonstrated that synthetic data sets could
be created without real data, based on analyst’s background knowledge, statistical models, or simulations.
This background knowledge comes from textbooks, historical conditions, statistical distributions, and
experience. Additionally, involving domain specialists in this process ensures that the synthetic data aligns
with real data, enabling assumptions about distributions and correlations when historical data is unavailable.
Research Gap and Problem Statement
Drilling inventory expresses an expensive asset in oil integrating companies, yet detailed investigations
into inventory KPIs are sparse, owing to limited availability of data for research purposes. Consequently,
generating a synthetic dataset became inevitable to allow researchers to develop innovative inventory
management approaches in oil operating companies, which might ultimately lead to identifying financial
issues and achieve substantial potential savings.
The introduced novel approach addresses the generation of a large-scale synthetic data set of
drilling inventory system of a medium-sized oil company, establishing a benchmark for future work on
investigations of drilling inventory optimization. Inventory management metrics were also evaluated on
the developed data set, to ensure its reliability. Data is open-source, codebase was developed using python,
and the final output is presented in PDF, which replicates actual reports of inventory transactions in oil
companies.
Motivation for Generating Synthetic Data.   Goldfarb and Tucker (2012) declared that digitization has
interconnected privacy policy with innovation policy, arguing that data privacy represents the tradeoff
between data-driven innovations and potential risks of data sharing. Bellovin et al. (2019) revealed that
perfect privacy is achieved when no data is shared, while optimum utility is achieved when data is not
perturbed. Few early studies implemented hypothetical data to depict the effectiveness of synthetic data sets
in drawing conclusions for SCM in the oil industry (Montagna and Cafaro, 2019; Marcellino and Sichman,
2010).
Review of Inventory Management
Stevens (1989) delineated supply chain (SC) as a series of interconnected activities concerned with material
planning and information flow, extending to supplier and manufacturer management, purchasing, facilities
planning, and customer service, with an ultimate objective of balancing the tradeoff between high customer
satisfaction, wide product range in market, low financial working capital, low inventory investment, and low
Downloaded from http://onepetro.org/SPEATCE/proceedings-pdf/24ATCE/24ATCE/D031S042R003/3603481/spe-220950-ms.pdf/1 by Schlumberger Oilfield UK Plc user on 27 September 2025

SPE-220950-MS
unit cost. Mentzer et al. (2001) defined SC as a network comprising at least three entities, either individuals
or organizations. Houlihan (1988) differentiated the emerging concept of SCM from traditional materials
control, mentioning that SCM integrates diverse perspectives to achieve joint strategic decision making,
unlike classical SC, which was viewed as a single process with separate responsibilities.
In year 2015, American Production Inventory Control Society (APICS) defined inventory or stock as
items used to support production (raw material and work-in-process), activities (maintenance, repair, and
operating supplies), and customer service (finished goods and spare parts). Vrat (2014) identified inventory
as the central theme of SCM, describing it as idle resources that tie-up financial capital without being
productive. Aro-Gordon and Gupte (2016) demonstrated four types of inventories, known as slow-moving,
fast-moving, dormant, and obsolete. Ivanov et al. (2021) outlined that inventory is typically an expensive
asset, which could hold up to 50% of total invested capital of companies where the fundamental costs
are outlined as carrying costs, ordering costs, and shortage costs. Naddor (1966) mentioned that inventory
problems arise when a decision is needed to initiate a purchase order – determining when to buy – and set
reorder points – determining how much to buy. Inventory should be addressed as a symptom of problems,
which could impact the entire SC unless root causes are investigated, as naïve inventory reduction can lead
to shortage of items and extra procurement costs.
Inventory Problems and Analysis
Wild (2017) pointed out that inventory could suffer from obsolescence, slow systems, and extra stock due
to inefficiencies in determining accurate quantities of a purchase order (PO), identifying reliable safety
stock, forecasting market changes, and poorly defined responsibilities. Shenoy and Rosas (2018) depicted
the key issues of inventory as demand, replenishment lead time, inventory level, review time, lifetime, and
repairability. Ivanov et al. (2021) mentioned that optimizing the trade-off between service levels and costs
is the challenging core of inventory management.
Role of Analytics.   Naddor (1966) stressed the necessity of inventory models, which establish mathematical
relationships between holding costs, stockout, and ordering costs. Hausman (2001) demonstrated that
analytics allow companies to measure tied funds and compare inventory levels across different categories.
Kleijnen and Smits (2003) distinguished between theoretical SCM evaluation metrics and those used
in practical business. Souza (2014) underscored the critical role of data analysis, exact algorithms, and
approximate formulas in designing inventory policies, particularly forecast patterns, replenishment lead
times, service levels, and holding costs. Kache and Seuring (2017) showed that the origin and type of
information are fundamental dimensions for big data analytics in SCM. Nguyen et al. (2018) examined the
role of big data analytics – volume, variety, velocity, veracity, and value – in optimizing inventory control
dynamics, warehouse operations, order picking problems, and storage assignment.
Inventory Business Metrics.   Gunasekaran et al. (2001) listed key performance metrics of inventory
systems and classified them based on their financial impact. Those metrics could be categorized into three
levels: 1) strategic, including total cash flow, return on investment, delivery lead time, cycle time, and
customer query time, 2) tactical, encompassing demand and forecast, product cycle time, and transportation
costs, and 3) operational, involving carrying costs, manufacturing costs, and capacity utilization. Hausman
(2001) depicted two primary metrics for inventory evaluation: inventory valuation, representing total
monetary worth of unsold materials, and inventory turns, which are connected with stock flow and time
supply. He also highlighted the importance of speed metrics, such as cycle time and cash conversion cycle,
for effective inventory monitoring.
Vrat (2004) delineated inventory policies as the standard operating procedures for inventory models,
highlighting the common policies such as 1) economic order quantity (EOQ) – Reorder point (ROP) policy,
2) periodic review inventory policy, and 3) optional replenishment policy. Aro-Gordon and Gupte (2016)
distinguished the need for precise calculations of diverse stock levels for effective inventory management,

SPE-220950-MS
The first step focuses on initiating a drilling schedule for a hypothetical company and generating dates
of annual drilling campaigns over a period of 20 years. The second step pertains to processing POs and
receiving the requested equipment into inventory, aligning materials requisitions with drilling plans to mimic
real oilfield operations. The third step frames a high-level design for the inventory system, emphasizing
major categories as well as establishing a temporal connection between drilling operations and materials
issuance to support operations accordingly. The fourth step reveals an iterative approach to generate the
subclasses of each item, incorporating new high-tech items annually besides accounting for anomalous
transactions. The final step validates, evaluates, and checks the logical consistency of the output data set.
Phase I: Drilling Schedule Blueprint
This stage serves as the foundation of the workflow, constructing a database for a hypothetical oil operating
company, involved in drilling activities for over 20 years. Synthetic dataset was constructed on the premise
that inventory data, purchase orders, and transactions history are linked to the number of wells drilled each
year. Typically, there is a direct correlation between oil prices and the number of wells drilled. Nonetheless,
other factors such as investment decisions, economic factors, cost of production, and regulatory environment
must also be considered. In this study, the first attribute was solely considered to construct the drilling
schedule spanning 20 years, resulting in a total of more than 200 generated synthetic drilled wells.
Initiate Drilling Database.   The process started with correlating the annual number of wells drilled to the
average yearly oil prices. It was observed that economic growth, supply chain disruptions, and commodity
prices impacted the US dollar inflation rates. Consequently, inflation-adjusted oil prices (Fig. 2a) were
introduced to accurately estimate yearly drilling activities, factoring inflation rate in oil prices over the past
20 years. It was evident that inflation rates, compared to the current status, varied drastically from 0.78 in
the year 2000 to 0.049 in the year 2022.
As shown in (Fig. 2a), the inflation-adjusted oil prices in years 2006 and 2022 are almost typical,
although the actual oil prices in both years differed by more than 42%. This underscores the significance
of considering inflation rates in oil prices and its impact on the number of wells drilled annually. It could
be noticed that the persistent pattern of the synthetic data closely mirrors the actual trend of wells drilled
globally from the year 2004 to the year 2022 (Fig. 2b). For the developed medium-sized company, it was
observed that the annual drilling activities (Fig. 2c) reached the lowest in the year 2003 with only 4 wells,
while peaked in 2008 with 14 wells, followed by a drop, which was impacted by the economic crisis.
Similarly, it was observed that the number of wells drilled in year 2020 dropped to 5 wells, adversely
impacted by the COVID-19 pandemic.

It could be observed that the catalogue of completion equipment or accessories has the highest number of
receipt transactions – almost double that of any other category–of the stock, due to the extensive variety of
completion equipment, the potential of POs splitting among different vendors, and varying delivery time
for items based on manufacturer availability. In contrast, tubular receipts usually arrive in batches
Craft Annual Purchase Orders Distribution.   A conservative approach was adopted, which assumes that
there should be at least one received order and at most six received orders of material requisitions every year.
A set of probabilities was presumed for the main six categories, each with a 0.10 probability of reception,
except for completion accessories, which were assigned a 0.50 probability, i.e. the likelihood of receiving
completion equipment is equal to the combined probabilities of receiving all other material. This process
was randomly generated, accounting for the possibility of materials being received in multiple shipments
and the fact that some materials might not need to be purchased annually. Distribution of yearly POs and
dates of materials entries into the inventory system (Fig. 3c) followed an iterative approach, which permitted
complete randomness and considered the current status of the inventory system. X-mas tree, for example,
experience 65 entries across the history of stock transactions, with an average of 3 entries per year, and a
minimum of 1 entry achieved in five distinct years and a maximum of 6 entries achieved once in year 2012.
Conversely, wellhead equipment achieved a maximum of 5 entries in years 2006 and 2007 only.
Furthermore, seasonal patterns of POs were observed in (Fig. 3d), which could be influenced by various
factors, including adjustments to the drilling schedule, weather conditions – especially for offshore rigs
affected by wind speed, wave height, and swell – market demand, regulatory requirements, and operational
constraints
Phase III: Inventory Framework
At this stage, a broad outline of the inventory system was presented, highlighting the general transactions
of issues and receipts that a drilling inventory might experience. Up to this point, inventory items are still
being considered as six primary groups, without defining subclasses. This was deliberately done, to prevent
any potential bias or inaccuracies that might arise from dealing with detailed inventory aspects.
Develop Framework for Material Requisition.   The process started by defining a description for each
main category followed by a suffix indicating the batch number issued to a single rig site. For instance, a
drilled well might require multiple n deliveries of drilling tubulars to the site, based on well engineering
design and the number of sections to be drilled. This would result in multiple issuances to the rig labeled as
drilling-tubular#1, drilling-tubular#2, and so on, up to drilling-tubular#n. The adopted approach was applied
across all the six main categories, with the maximum number of issuances and returns (Table-1) to each
rig generated randomly according to constraints derived from real-world experience of drilling operations.
This ensured that the developed system could realistically simulate rig conditions.

As an illustrative example, it can be seen that the number of returns for each item per well follows a
consistent pattern, where a value is randomly selected between a minimum and a maximum. The minimum
is a random value between zero and the higher of one or (number of issues minus two), while the maximum
is the number of issues plus one. For instance, for drilling tubulars, the number of times they are issued to
a well would be a randomly generated number between 2 and 5, assuming it is 4. For the same item, the
number of returns would be a randomly generated value between a random value between 0 and 2 against
5. If this random value is 2, then for this well, there would be issuances of drilling-tubular#1, drilling-
tubular#2, drilling-tubular#3, drilling-tubular#4, and returns of drilling-tubular#1 and drilling-tubular#2.
Statistical insights from issue and return transactions are depicted in (Table-2). It is clear that drilling
tubular are the most frequently issued items to rig, while completion tubulars are the least. This could be
attributed to the fact that operational problems typically occur during the drilling phase as opposed to the
completion phase. Such problems might result in changes in well planning, non-productive time, and other
problems, which could adversely impact the material transactions to and from the rig. It is obvious that the
trend in issuances of most categories aligns with their return transactions, yet with slightly lower means,
indicating that more items are issued than returned.

Design the Logic of Material Issuance to RIG.   Overall, this step creates a logic that synchronizes inventory
transactions with operations at rig site. Referring to the six main categories of drilling inventory, each
issuance to the rig would be temporally correlated to the spud-in date, besides being linked to the issuance
of other material to rig site. For example, drilling tubular of conductor would be the first equipment to be
issued to the rig site. In some cases, they might not be issued, assuming that the well is sidetracked and
already have conductor and surface casings in place. Similarly, casing size of 7" should only be issued to
the rig provided that casing size of 9 5/8" has already been issued or provided that it is a sidetrack in a well
that was originally designed with 9 5/8" casing. Likewise, different sizes of drilling wellheads should be
issued along with drilling tubular. It is important to notice that the introduced logic addresses drilling wells
solely, and that it does not apply to workover wells, which would neglect items#1 to #3.
Time-based sequence of inventory SKUs transactions was designed based on practical experience and
well engineering principles (Table-3). This process was applied to both issues and returns for each item.
First items to be issued to the rig are conductors and surface casings, which are assumed to happen within
two weeks from rig spud-in on a well. In contrast, wellheads are temporally associated with the issuance
of drilling tubulars, such that they typically occur within a range of one week before or one week after the
issuance of drilling tubular. For returns to the inventory system, it was assumed that SKUs, whether new,
Downloaded from http://onepetro.org/SPEATCE/proceedings-pdf/24ATCE/24ATCE/D031S042R003/3603481/spe-220950-ms.pdf/1 by Schlumberger Oilfield UK Plc user on 27 September 2025

tech items into inventory and permits the presence of obsolete items. In addition, it incorporates exceptional
transactions such as inspection, loan, sales, and junk.
Create Customized and Multi-Versioned SKUs.   This section centers on the dates of entries and the
description of SKUs. Each category is divided into subclasses, reflecting items with different technical
specifications, manufacturers, or varying conditions of use. Constraints were established, so that each unique
item in the inventory shall be broken down into a minimum of 5 subclasses and a maximum of 10 subclasses,
following a left-skewed exponential distribution. More constraints were applied to replicate real operations
and maintain the introduced logic. According to this initial setup, the original stock items were defined,
and their entry dates were projected. It was assumed that some items were already part of the inventory in
the year 2000, with no information about their earlier history of transactions. Therefore, it was presumed
that the entry date for those items was the first day in the year 2000 and random quantities for each item
were assigned.
The objective of the introduced constraints was to guarantee that a subclass item cannot be issued before
it had been already purchased and received through a PO. Similarly, any item returned to the stock must
have been issued to a rig earlier. In addition, an iterative criterion ensured the possible presence of obsolete
SKUs, where each subclassed item was associated to a skewed probability of 0.15 of being obsolete in a
given year. This approach simulates real scenarios in inventories, where an item is assumed obsolete of the
company decided to discontinue their use in operations for any reason.
In order to integrate high-tech or new specifications of items in the inventory system, the period from
2000 to 2023 was split into four groups, with splitters randomly selected, such that each segment could
cover a duration ranging between 5 and 9 years. For each segment, a random number of the newly added
items was estimated based on a normal distribution. This iterative process was applied to each subclassed
item. For a straightforward example, assume the first splitter is a value randomly chosen from years between
2002 to 2004 and the second splitter is selected randomly from year 2007 to year 2009. The generated
segment could be supposedly picked from 2003 to 2009, covering a span of 6 years. Based on this segment,
the number of new items designated to a unique class would be randomly determined to be between 2 and 4.
This reproducible approach resulted in the generation of 500 unique SKUs for drilling inventory,
distributed across diverse categories with unique number of entry dates (Table-4).

the last entry dates of items in the stock are left and right – skewed respectively (Fig. 5a). Furthermore,
the average inventory retention time, which represents how long SKUs remain in the stock before being

first received from a purchase order, while the last entry date is indicating the most recent time an item had
been added to the stock. It could be viewed that several items have their last entry date earlier than 2020,
suggesting that they are no longer in demand by the company, indicating potential obsolescence. Similarly,
the majority of first entry dates correspond to the year 2000, which refers to items that have already been

Starting with conductor and surface casing category, the proposed design allowed tubulars to be issued
to the rig as a batch that might consist of different casing sizes assuming that the rig had enough space
to accommodate them. Given the short timeframe in running the surface casings, various combinations
were considered. This framework also included the likelihood of sending the same casing size in multi-
batches with a probability of 0.15, accounting for potential operational challenges. Similarly, the same logic
was applied to drilling tubular of intermediate and production casings, completion tubular, accessories,
and wellheads. This iterative process was crucial for maintaining scalability and sustainability of inventory
transactions. It verified the size compatibility of drilling tubulars with tubing tubulars, for example, a 5"
liner cannot accommodate a 4" tubing. Likewise, it confirmed that the sizes of completion accessories with
tubing and casing tubulars are matching, such as a 7" × 3 ½" permanent or retrievable packer not being used
with other tubular sizes in the well. This process was meticulously designed to guarantee that transactions
adhered to the established logic.
Furthermore, this design considered dual producers, gravel pack, and artificial lift designs. Also,
equipment that is usually received on a consignment basis has been included in this approach. Anomalous
transactions were designed to occur rarely in the inventory with a 0.30 probability for any item. Items are
assumed to have a possibility of being junked if 1) they had not experienced any type of transactions over
N years, where N is a randomly generated number following a normal distribution mean of 10 years and
a standard deviation of 5 years, or 2) damaged while movement, with a 0.25 probability for less than 0.15
of the returned equipment from the rig. In addition, loan transactions were designed with a criterion that
loaned items should return to the inventory within a randomly selected period between 3 and 12 months.
Furthermore, used items that return to inventory were classified into condition-B – items that are functional
but might have minor defects from being used at most once during the operation – and condition-C – items
that are still functional after being used multiple times. Items that are sold to other companies are priced
based on the latest value of the same category in the stock.
Phase IV: Database Validation
At this stage, the data set has already been constructed successfully. This step introduces extra inventory data
and reviews the workflow of the synthetic dataset and ensures meeting predefined conditions and criteria.
Construct Database.   In this step, the quantities of each SKU received annually were designed according to
the drilling campaigns planned in the following year. In contrast, the prices of each SKU were determined
based on the current market knowledge of the drilling SKUs prices. Criteria that account for US dollar
inflation rate, depreciation, and supply chain impacts were considered to estimate the historical prices
of SKUs, when they initially entered the inventory system. In addition, Price fluctuations were also
accommodated into the inventory tables.
SKU identifiers have been assigned to each unique item in the stock. Transaction number and requisition
orders have been associated to SKUs. Analytical accounts responsible for transactions were added. In
addition, columns of balance and value have been added. The database was organized in such a way that
each item could be extracted into a separate table, providing a summary of each single transaction that
it underwent. Overview of item technical description, item criticality, and warehouse locations were also
introduced for each item. Data could be extracted either in PDF or Excel formats to facilitate further analysis.
Check that Orders Follow Logical Processes.   To ensure seamless transactions, constraints are checked
systematically throughout the process. The ratio between number of issues for dependent SKUs was
validated to be within +/-0.10, and the receipt-to-issue ratio was set to be within an acceptable range between
1:3 and 1:8 (Table-6). For instance, 9 5/8" casing issues should closely match to 13 3/8" casing issues, given
that both are likely used in subsequent section in drilling operations. Similarly, 5" casing issues should be
less than 7" casings because the former would not run in a well unless the latter was already in place. In
addition, 7" casings could be issued more than 9 5/8" casings, accounting for sidetracked wells that already
Downloaded from http://onepetro.org/SPEATCE/proceedings-pdf/24ATCE/24ATCE/D031S042R003/3603481/spe-220950-ms.pdf/1 by Schlumberger Oilfield UK Plc user on 27 September 2025

have intermediate casings installed. It was also presumed that any item in inventory should be issued within
a maximum of 5 years from its last entry date, however, exceptions were allowed with a 0.20 probability.

Results
In this section, the results from the methodology are presented, displaying the output dataset constructed
at each step. This section validates the robustness of the introduced synthetic dataset, showcasing how it
accurately mimics real field operations data.

are generated and correlated to the drilling dates. (Table-7d) shows the combined dataset that includes
diverse transaction types including anomalies. (Table-7e) demonstrates the final constructed dataset for a

Final Database.   Similar to the sample presented in (Table-7e), each of the 500 unique SKUs is presented
in separate tables, which are then combined into a single or multi accessible PDF files. The primary
objective was to create a database that resembles real drilling inventory databases, encompassing all
potential transactions while accounting for the possible human and system errors in data recording. The
provided example demonstrates an SKU that has been purchased for the first time to the inventory system
in 2010. Extensive operations have been carried until 2015, resulting in more than 20 various transactions
as stated. The reception-to-issue ratio was 1:3, which could be reasonable given the high demand for the
item. It could be observed that multiple issuances have been transacted to the system, while a single return
transaction was sufficient to accommodate them all, due to the possibility that there might be no return from
certain rigs, when the items had been issued precisely as per the well engineering design without any back-
up. In addition, inspection for the returned items has been factored in for through the allowance of junk
transaction, which involves scrapping some items that returned from Rig in a damaged status. In the year
2016, it could be observed that there was a batch of the items, delivered on two consecutive dates with a
six-month delay accounting for potential manufacturing and shipment problems. Similarly, it is apparent
that there were no active transactions for the item in subsequent years, except for one issuance in 2020,
indicating a possible flaw in the demand planning for the inventory system. This is corroborated by the sale
of a large quantity later in year 2021. Following this, the item did not experience any further transaction
despite being in the stock, suggesting that this module of this SKU might be outdated or obsolete according
to the company standards.
Discussion
In this section, some insights from the final output dataset are presented. In addition, the role of data analytics
in identifying pain points, particularly in major transactions of issues, returns, and receipts, was emphasized.
In (Fig.6), timeseries analysis is showcased with (Fig. 6a) classifying the annual transactions by category,
where seasonality could be viewed as expected, affected by oil prices and demand. To further demonstrate, a
monthly timeseries of each transaction type was introduced in (Fig. 6b), highlighting significant seasonality
Downloaded from http://onepetro.org/SPEATCE/proceedings-pdf/24ATCE/24ATCE/D031S042R003/3603481/spe-220950-ms.pdf/1 by Schlumberger Oilfield UK Plc user on 27 September 2025
23:
SPE-220950-MS
23
into inventory dimensions, and identifying latent challenges such as stock count accuracy, scrap stock,
underutilization of annual capital allocation, and excess inventory surplus.
In addition, it is necessary to investigate data driven analysis, which could be a key contributor to optimize
inventory management. Comprehensive approaches of aging inventory, safety stock, service levels, and
turnover ratios are necessary to be addressed, in order to optimize inventory policy, demand forecasting,
and decision-making support.
As mentioned earlier, future contributions to the source of this open-source dataset are essential as a way
forward to advance the potential for innovative approaches in drilling inventory management.
Conclusion
This paper presents a novel approach, which produces a synthetic dataset for drilling inventory systems,
using a replicable iterative approach. Codebase was made open source to permit reproducibility and enable
future innovative research on the constructed dataset. Financial analytics were briefly outlined, since they
were a minor purpose of the research. To the authors’ knowledge, this is the first open-source synthetic
dataset for drilling inventory systems in the oil and gas industry.
The generated inventory system reflects real transactions, as thoroughly explained in the results and
discussion sections. The introduced dataset consisted of nearly 500 unique SKUs, varying from drilling
tubular to completion equipment, accessories, and production Xmas trees. Anomalous transactions, such as
junk, sales, and loans were incorporated, while potential human and system errors were accounted for using
random probabilities. A systematic approach was followed to estimate SKUs prices over time and estimate
the quantities required for each drilled well.
This dataset is intended to serve as a benchmark for future research into the role of data analytics and
machine learning in optimizing the performance of drilling inventories. This would allow the calculation
of different inventory metrics, such as cash flow, in a drilling inventory system that simulates real data.
Further research could lead to innovative approaches for inventory management, which could ultimately
be applied to actual inventory data from oil companies, potentially resulting in significant cost savings in
millions of dollars.
